chief_complaint: sudden weakness in right arm and slurred speech this morning, lasted 20 minutes then resolved
hpi_details: sudden onset right arm weakness and dysarthria, lasted approximately 20 minutes then completely resolved, no headache, no visual changes, no loss of consciousness, first episode ever
ideas: thinks she was just tired and stressed
concerns: daughter insisted she come in — she didn't think it was serious
expectations: wants to go home quickly as she has grandchildren to look after
medications: amlodipine 5mg OD, atorvastatin 40mg ON, metformin 500mg BD
social_hx: non-smoker, non-drinker, lives with husband, independent in all ADLs, retired teacher

reference_soap.subjective: 67F with HTN, hypercholesterolaemia, T2DM presents with sudden-onset right arm weakness and dysarthria lasting ~20 min then completely resolving. First episode. No headache or visual loss.
reference_soap.objective: Neurological exam (currently normal), BP both arms, ECG (AF?), bloods: FBC, glucose, lipids, coag, CT head (rule out haemorrhage), carotid Doppler, echo if indicated.
reference_soap.assessment: TIA — high stroke risk. ABCD2 score required. DDx: focal seizure, hypoglycaemia, hemiplegic migraine, Todd's paresis.
reference_soap.plan: Admit for stroke unit monitoring (high early stroke risk post-TIA), dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 300mg + clopidogrel 75mg), statin, BP control, carotid imaging, ECG monitoring x72h for paroxysmal AF, patient education on FAST warning signs.

learning_objectives: Characterise TIA: sudden onset, brief duration, complete resolution
Calculate ABCD2 score and explain stroke urgency
Counsel patient who is reluctant to stay in hospital
